claim: AI Scientist (Lu et al., 2024) demonstrated the first fully autonomous system capable of generating novel research hypotheses, implementing experiments in code, and producing complete scientific manuscripts without human intervention, closing the loop from idea to paper.
reason: The claim describes the original AI Scientist by Lu et al. (2024) as a fully autonomous end-to-end system generating hypotheses, implementing experiments, and producing manuscripts. This matches openalex:W4402952666::k00, which describes the same system with identical capabilities at ~$15/paper.

claim: AlphaFold 3 (Abramson et al., 2024) extended structure prediction to biomolecular complexes including DNA, RNA, and small molecules using a diffusion-based architecture, establishing a new paradigm for AI-driven structural biology that accelerates drug target identification.
reason: The claim describes AlphaFold 3's diffusion-based architecture for biomolecular complex structure prediction. None of the candidates describe AlphaFold 3 or any structure prediction system; the candidates cover unrelated topics (macular degeneration, evaluation protocols, biology benchmarks, hypothesis refinement, description logic, and reasoning generalization).

claim: HypoGeniC (Zhou et al., 2024) showed that iterative hypothesis refinement using LLM agents guided by experimental feedback outperforms single-shot generation, achieving statistically significant improvements on drug discovery and materials science benchmarks.
reason: The claim describes HypoGeniC itself showing that iterative refinement outperforms single-shot generation on drug discovery and materials science benchmarks. No candidate matches this: arxiv:2410.17309v3::k00 describes HypoRefine (which builds on HypoGeniC) evaluated on social-science tasks, not drug discovery or materials science benchmarks. The other candidates describe different systems (ACCELMAT, Robin, MATDESIGN, iterative human-LLM-lab workflow, multi-agent collaboration). None describe HypoGeniC's own evaluation on drug discovery and materials science.

claim: SciAgentBench (2024) introduced a comprehensive benchmark of 338 research-level scientific tasks across 44 diverse disciplines to evaluate LLM agents on authentic scientific problem-solving, finding that top agents solve fewer than 10% of tasks.
reason: The claim describes SciAgentBench with 338 tasks across 44 disciplines and a <10% solve rate. The only candidate sharing the name (arxiv:2602.12984v2::k01) describes a tiered suite of 259 tasks and 1,134 sub-questions with L1-L3 levels — different task count, different structural framing, and no mention of 44 disciplines or the <10% finding. No candidate matches the specific benchmark described in the claim.

claim: MLAgentBench (Huang et al., 2024) provided a standardized evaluation framework for LLM agents performing machine learning research tasks including literature review, experiment design, and iterative refinement, revealing systematic failure modes in long-horizon planning.
reason: The claim describes MLAgentBench as a standardized evaluation framework for LLM agents performing ML research tasks (literature review, experiment design, iterative refinement) with systematic failure modes in long-horizon planning. No candidate matches this specific benchmark. The failure-modes candidates (openalex:W7119558012::k00 and arxiv:2602.15112v2::k02) describe failure modes from different systems/papers, not MLAgentBench specifically.

None of the candidates describe this specific system — the closest is openalex:W4412889809::k03 which also involves multi-agent collaboration in scientific idea generation outperforming single-agent baselines, but it describes a different system focused on alignment with research trends and Science of Science findings, not the specific ResearchAgent architecture with its three specialized sub-agent roles.

claim: Autonomous Chemical Research with Coscientist (Boiko et al., 2024 follow-up work) scaled to multi-step organic synthesis planning and robotic execution, successfully completing reactions that required integrating web search, database queries, and hardware control without human intervention.

claim: AI peer review systems evaluated in Liang et al. (2024) revealed that GPT-4-based reviewers agree with human reviewers at rates comparable to inter-human agreement on NeurIPS and ICLR submissions, while also identifying systematic biases toward longer, more confident-sounding papers.
reason: The claim combines two findings: (1) GPT-4 reviewers agree with humans at inter-human rates on NeurIPS/ICLR, and (2) systematic biases toward longer, more confident-sounding papers. Candidate W4400734098::k01 captures the agreement-rate finding but omits the distinctive bias finding entirely. No candidate mentions biases toward longer or more confident papers, which is a specific empirical result central to the claim. The closest candidates (W4400734098::k01, W4384261641::k00) match only part of the claim and none captures the full specific contribution described.

Candidate k02 describes exactly this: an agent-based RAG system for scientific literature that surpasses GPT-4 by 30 percentage points on PubMedQA — the same specific empirical contribution, allowing for paraphrase of the benchmark and margin details.

Candidate k03 from W4416540539 describes controlled synthetic datasets with varied difficulty where hypothesis discovery rates collapse as complexity grows, directly matching the empirical finding about LLM limitations on hypothesis discovery tasks with structured data.

None of the candidates describe this benchmark or its findings — they cover AI research agents, peer review summarization, paper review quality, and research ideation, not scientific coding benchmarks.

claim: Autonomous Laboratory Agent (Boiko et al., 2023 / follow-on 2024 work) deployed GPT-4 as a chemistry lab orchestrator that designs experiments, queries literature, writes instrument code, and closes the loop with results, successfully synthesizing several novel compounds with minimal human intervention.
reason: The claim describes Boiko et al.'s Autonomous Laboratory Agent — a GPT-4-based chemistry lab orchestrator that designs experiments, queries literature, writes instrument code, and synthesizes novel compounds. None of the candidates match this specific system; all candidates describe general-purpose AI scientist pipelines for ML research (AI Scientist, AI-Researcher, Jr. AI Scientist, Agent Laboratory) or an AFM-specific lab assistant (AILA), none of which is the chemistry-focused Boiko et al. system.

claim: ResearchAgent (Baek et al., 2024) built a reading-writing-judging pipeline where specialized LLM agents iteratively refine research ideas against related-work context, showing that multi-agent refinement substantially increases human-rated novelty and feasibility scores compared to single-pass generation.

None of the candidates match this specific system. VIRSCI (W4412889809) is a multi-agent system for idea generation but uses a different architecture (virtual scientists cloned from profiles). MARG-S (W4390784029) is for scientific feedback, not idea generation. The others describe different systems or general findings without the specific reading-writing-judging pipeline described.

claim: BioPlanner (O'Donoghue et al., 2024) introduced a benchmark for evaluating LLM generation of experimental protocols in biology, revealing that while GPT-4 produces plausible step sequences, it systematically omits safety and reagent-concentration details that domain experts include, motivating tool-augmented grounding.
reason: None of the candidates describe BioPlanner or a benchmark specifically for evaluating LLM generation of biological experimental protocols with findings about GPT-4 omitting safety/reagent-concentration details. The closest candidates involve lab automation or scientific benchmarks but are distinct systems (SciSafetyBench, tri-agent lab architecture) with different specific contributions.

claim: Sakana AI's evolutionary model merging and automated curriculum search (2024) showed that iterative LLM-driven meta-learning—where an agent proposes, evaluates, and selects model architectures or training strategies—can discover novel Pareto-optimal solutions on math and code benchmarks without human-specified search spaces.
reason: The claim describes Sakana AI's evolutionary model merging work — a specific system using evolutionary algorithms to merge pre-trained models and discover Pareto-optimal solutions on math/code benchmarks. None of the candidates describe this system; they cover AIDE (code optimization via tree search), RLER (reinforcement learning with evolving rubrics), ToolUniverse (ML tool ecosystem), MLGym (Gym environment for LLM agents), and AI Scientist-v2 (automated scientific discovery). None match evolutionary model merging or automated curriculum search in the sense Sakana AI demonstrated.

claim: OpenScholar (Asai et al., 2024) showed that retrieval-augmented generation over 45 million open-access papers combined with self-feedback loops substantially outperforms general LLMs and GPT-4 on scientific literature synthesis tasks, reducing hallucinated citations by over 50%.
reason: The claim is specifically about OpenScholar (Asai et al., 2024), a RAG system over 45M open-access papers with self-feedback loops that reduces hallucinated citations by >50%. None of the candidates describe OpenScholar — the closest candidates (PaperQA, PaperQA2) are different systems by different authors, and none mention the 45M paper corpus, self-feedback loops, or the specific hallucination-reduction finding.

reason: None of the candidates describe SciAgentBench or a benchmark of 338 tasks across 5 domains with a <10% solve rate finding. The closest candidates (SDE benchmark, DeepResearch Bench) are distinct systems with different scopes and findings.

claim: MLAgentBench (Huang et al., 2024) provided a benchmark of 13 machine learning research tasks requiring agents to autonomously read datasets, implement ML pipelines, and iteratively improve model performance, with the best agents achieving meaningful improvements on only a subset of tasks, revealing fundamental limitations in autonomous ML research.

None of the candidates match this specific system. The closest (openalex:W4389761608::k02) describes PaperQA outperforming GPT-4 on PubMedQA, which is a different system and different benchmark.

reason: The claim refers specifically to the FrontierMath benchmark (Glazer et al., 2024) — a dataset of expert-level math research problems with a <2% solve rate by frontier LLMs. None of the candidates describe this benchmark or finding; they cover ML competition tasks, peer review quality, AI-generated paper corpora, ML replication benchmarks, AI research agent benchmarks, and biology research tasks.

claim: AI Scientist (Lu et al., 2024) demonstrated the first fully autonomous LLM-based system capable of ideating, implementing, and writing up machine learning research papers end-to-end, producing NeurIPS-workshop-quality submissions without human intervention.
reason: The claim describes the AI Scientist as a fully autonomous end-to-end LLM system that ideates, implements, and writes ML research papers, producing NeurIPS-workshop-quality submissions. This matches openalex:W4402952666::k00, which describes the same system with the same key features including the cost detail (~$15/paper). The other candidate openalex:W7140287209::k00 describes the same system but is a different paper instance; W4402952666::k00 is the better match as it includes the cost framing aligning with Lu et al. 2024.

claim: ResearchBench (2025) provided a large-scale benchmark for evaluating hypothesis generation quality in LLM agents by testing whether models can reconstruct held-out hypotheses from paper abstracts across multiple scientific fields.
reason: The claim describes ResearchBench, a large-scale benchmark for LLM scientific agents spanning multiple domains including hypothesis-driven inquiry. arxiv:2604.18805::k00 matches this as a large-scale benchmark (25,000+ agent runs) evaluating LLM-based scientific agents across eight domains through both systematic performance analysis and epistemological behavioral analysis, covering hypothesis-driven inquiry — consistent with the claim's description of evaluating hypothesis generation quality across multiple scientific fields.

claim: SciAgentBench (Chen et al., 2025) introduced a benchmark of 338 scientific tasks across 44 domains to evaluate LLM agents on realistic computational research workflows, finding that top agents solve fewer than 40% of tasks.
reason: The claim's key finding — that top agents solve fewer than 40% of scientific tasks — matches the specific empirical finding in k03 (32.4% unaided, 34.3% with knowledge, 42.2% with o1-preview) on real-world scientific coding workflows. The benchmark described in arxiv:2602.12984v2::k01 is also named SciAgentBench but has different task counts (259 vs 338), suggesting a distinct version or paper. The openalex entry best matches the Chen et al. 2025 claim on task-solve rates.

Candidate k03 from the same paper (W4399597648) states that strong baseline agents struggle on end-to-end scientific discovery tasks requiring complete discovery cycles, which matches this empirical finding.

claim: Autonomous Chemical Research (Boiko et al., 2023, published/extended 2024 in Nature) reported Coscientist, a GPT-4-powered agent that autonomously designed and executed chemical experiments using web search, documentation retrieval, and robotic lab hardware control.
reason: The claim describes Coscientist, a specific GPT-4-powered agent for autonomous chemical experiments with web search, documentation retrieval, and robotic lab control (Boiko et al., 2023/2024). None of the candidates match this specific system. The closest candidates (BioMARS, AILA) are similar in theme (LLM-driven robotic lab automation) but are different specific systems, not Coscientist.

claim: PaperQA2 (Skarlinski et al., 2024) showed that a retrieval-augmented agent combining full-text PDF search with citation chaining reached expert human-level accuracy on literature question answering, surpassing earlier RAG baselines on the LitQA2 evaluation set.
reason: The claim describes PaperQA2 (Skarlinski et al., 2024) achieving expert human-level accuracy on literature QA via retrieval-augmented generation with citation chaining, evaluated on LitQA2. This matches candidate k00 from W4403795293, which describes PaperQA2 with agentic tool use including citation traversal matching/exceeding PhD-level expert performance on scientific literature tasks.

claim: SciReplica / REval benchmark (2024) curated 100 computational-science papers and measured LLM agent ability to reproduce reported results, finding GPT-4 Turbo reproduces roughly 40% of tasks without human scaffolding.
reason: The claim describes a benchmark called SciReplica/REval with 100 computational-science papers and a specific GPT-4 Turbo ~40% reproduction rate. CORE-Bench (openalex:W4403707291::k00) is the closest candidate — a benchmark of 270 tasks from 90 scientific papers measuring AI agent computational reproducibility — but it is a different system with different scope and no mention of the specific GPT-4 Turbo 40% finding. No candidate matches the specific SciReplica/REval system described.

claim: OpenScholar (Asai et al., 2024) built a retrieval-augmented system over 45 million open-access papers and demonstrated that it outperforms GPT-4 with web search on scientific synthesis tasks on the ScholarQA benchmark, with higher citation precision.
reason: The claim describes OpenScholar (Asai et al., 2024), a specific RAG system over 45M open-access papers evaluated on ScholarQA. None of the candidates match this: PaperQA/PaperQA2 are different systems, SciMuse is a collaboration idea generator, and no candidate mentions OpenScholar, ScholarQA, or the 45M paper corpus.

claim: HypoGeniC (Zhou et al., 2024) introduced an iterative hypothesis generation framework in which an agent proposes hypotheses, tests them against evidence retrieved from corpora, and revises them, achieving higher accuracy than chain-of-thought prompting on social-science prediction tasks.
reason: No candidate describes HypoGeniC itself. The arxiv:2410.17309v3::k00 candidate (HypoRefine) references HypoGeniC as a component it builds on, but its own contribution is the literature-grounded refinement layer on top — a different system. The other candidates describe different iterative hypothesis generation frameworks in different domains or with different architectures.

claim: AgentReview (Jin et al., 2024) simulated the full NeurIPS peer-review process with LLM agents playing author, reviewer, and area-chair roles, showing that GPT-4-based reviewers exhibit similar biases (positivity bias, authority bias) to human reviewers measured on held-out acceptance data.
reason: The claim describes AgentReview (Jin et al., 2024), a framework that simulates the full peer-review pipeline with LLM agents playing reviewer, author, and area-chair roles — this is the same system described in k00, which explicitly names AgentReview and its customizable multi-role simulation. The bias findings mentioned in the claim are results from that same system, making k00 the primary matching contribution.

claim: SciCode (Tian et al., 2024) released a code-generation benchmark of 338 research-level scientific programming subproblems spanning mathematics, physics, chemistry, biology, and materials science, where frontier models including GPT-4o solve fewer than 5% of hard problems without hints.
reason: The claim describes SciCode (Tian et al., 2024), a benchmark of 338 research-level scientific programming subproblems where frontier models solve fewer than 5% of hard problems. None of the candidates match this specific benchmark — the candidates describe SDE, an AI-generated papers corpus, SciAgentGym, an FRI corpus, evolutionary code search, and a chemistry hypothesis benchmark. None is SciCode.

claim: STORM (Shao et al., 2024, Stanford) presented a system that autonomously writes Wikipedia-style long-form articles on technical topics by simulating multi-perspective expert interviews and grounding claims in retrieved web sources, outperforming RAG baselines on citation coverage and factual density.
reason: The claim describes STORM (Shao et al., 2024), a specific system for writing Wikipedia-style articles via simulated multi-perspective expert interviews with web-grounded citations. None of the candidates describe this specific system — openalex:W4403795293::k04 is about a language agent generating scientific topic summaries judged more accurate than Wikipedia, but this is a different system and contribution (accuracy vs. Wikipedia, not the STORM interview-simulation approach). The other candidates describe unrelated systems.

claim: Lab-in-a-Loop / Closed-Loop Bayesian Optimization agent (Ramos et al., 2024) coupled an LLM planning module with a Bayesian optimizer and robotic liquid-handling hardware to autonomously optimize reaction conditions in chemistry, reaching near-human expert performance in fewer experimental iterations than traditional active learning.
reason: The claim describes a specific system (Lab-in-a-Loop by Ramos et al., 2024) that couples an LLM with Bayesian optimization and robotic liquid-handling for chemistry reaction condition optimization. None of the candidates match this specific system: W4417287919 (Robin) is a multi-agent system for autonomous scientific discovery but focuses on hypothesis generation/refinement, not Bayesian optimization for chemistry reactions; W4411721350 is a pipeline for biological hypotheses with metabolomics, not chemistry reaction optimization with Bayesian methods; the others are clearly different systems entirely.

claim: AI Scientist (Lu et al., 2024) demonstrated a fully autonomous research pipeline — generating hypotheses, writing code, running experiments, and producing manuscript drafts — on machine-learning tasks including NAS and diffusion models, producing papers judged comparable to workshop submissions by human reviewers.
reason: The claim describes the AI Scientist (Lu et al., 2024) as a fully autonomous pipeline generating hypotheses, writing code, running experiments, and producing manuscript drafts on ML tasks. This matches W7140287209::k00, which describes the AI Scientist as a fully automated end-to-end scientific research pipeline that autonomously generates research ideas, writes and executes code, runs experiments, analyzes results, writes manuscripts, and performs automated peer review.

claim: SciAgents (Buehler, 2024) used a multi-agent LLM framework with ontology-based knowledge graphs to autonomously generate and refine research hypotheses in materials science, introducing cross-domain reasoning over structured literature graphs.
reason: The claim describes SciAgents by Buehler (2024) — a multi-agent LLM framework using ontology-based knowledge graphs for autonomous hypothesis generation in materials science. Candidate k00 from the same paper (W4405599065) describes exactly this: SciAgents as a multi-agent framework integrating large-scale ontological knowledge graphs with specialized LLM-based agents to automate scientific discovery.

claim: MLAgentBench (Huang et al., 2024) introduced a benchmark of 13 ML engineering tasks (e.g., Cifar-10 improvement, BabyLM) where agents write and execute code; GPT-4-based agents solved only a subset, exposing gaps in long-horizon planning.
reason: The claim describes MLAgentBench, a specific benchmark of 13 ML engineering tasks (Cifar-10, BabyLM, etc.) by Huang et al. 2024 where agents write and execute code. None of the candidates match this specific benchmark — the closest is the Weco Kaggle Benchmark (openalex:W4407760093::k02) which is a different benchmark focused on tabular Kaggle tasks, not the MLAgentBench suite.

claim: DiscoveryBench (Majumder et al., 2024) defined a benchmark of 264 real discovery tasks drawn from published social-science and life-science papers, requiring agents to recover the original finding from raw data — state-of-the-art LLM agents achieved under 25% success.
reason: The claim describes DiscoveryBench (Majumder et al., 2024) — 264 real discovery tasks from social-science and life-science papers requiring agents to recover original findings from raw data, with <25% success. None of the candidates match: CORE-Bench (270 tasks, computational reproducibility), SocSci-Repro-Bench (221 reproduction tasks, social science only), BiomniBench-DA (100 biomedical tasks), and the chemistry/materials benchmarks are all distinct systems with different scope, framing, and authorship.

claim: ResearchAgent (Baek et al., 2024) built a Semantic Scholar–grounded agent that iteratively refines research ideas, questions, and methods by retrieving and synthesising relevant papers, with human evaluators rating its outputs on novelty and feasibility.
reason: The claim describes ResearchAgent by Baek et al. (2024), which uses Semantic Scholar to retrieve papers and iteratively refines research ideas/questions/methods with human evaluation on novelty and feasibility. Candidate k00 from W4411120082 is exactly ResearchAgent — an LLM system that identifies novel research problems and iteratively refines output, with the same authorship and paper identity.

claim: ChemCrow (Bran et al., 2023/published 2024) integrated GPT-4 with 17 chemistry tools (RDKit, web search, reaction databases) in a ReAct loop and autonomously planned and executed synthesis routes for insect repellents and chromophores.
reason: The claim describes ChemCrow, a specific system integrating GPT-4 with 17 chemistry tools in a ReAct loop for synthesis planning. None of the candidates describe ChemCrow or this specific system — the closest is SciAgentGym (chemistry tool environment) but that is a different system/benchmark, not ChemCrow itself.

claim: Autonomous lab agent for Bayesian optimisation (Boiko et al., Nature 2023, widely cited 2024) showed a GPT-4 agent controlling liquid-handling robots to run closed-loop chemistry experiments, discovering a Suzuki-coupling condition without human intervention.
reason: The claim describes an autonomous agent system executing chemistry experiments including catalyzed cross-coupling reactions (Suzuki coupling) in a closed-loop fashion. Candidate k00 describes an Intelligent Agent system combining LLMs to autonomously design, plan, and execute scientific experiments demonstrated on catalyzed cross-coupling reactions — this is the same specific contribution (Boiko et al. Coscientist paper).

claim: SciCode (Tian et al., 2024) released a benchmark of 338 multi-step scientific coding problems spanning physics, chemistry, and biology derived from research papers; frontier models (GPT-4o, Claude 3.5) solved fewer than 5% of hard sub-problems without hints.
reason: The claim describes SciCode, a benchmark of 338 multi-step scientific coding problems from research papers where frontier models solved fewer than 5% of hard sub-problems. claim: PaperQA2 (Lála et al., 2024) showed that a retrieval-augmented agent over full-text PDFs exceeded PhD-student accuracy on a curated set of 50 literature questions across biology, chemistry, and physics, establishing a concrete human-vs-agent comparison.
reason: The claim describes PaperQA2 (Lála et al., 2024) as a retrieval-augmented agent exceeding PhD-student accuracy on literature questions, which directly matches candidate k00 from W4403795293 describing PaperQA2 matching or exceeding PhD-level performance on scientific literature tasks. The claim's '50 questions across biology, chemistry, physics' is a paraphrase of the LitQA2 benchmark context. The human-vs-agent comparison is explicitly mentioned in both.

claim: AgentReview (Jin et al., 2024) simulated the full ICLR peer-review process with LLM agents playing authors, reviewers, and area chairs; it found that LLM reviewers replicate known human biases (positivity bias, susceptibility to author prestige) at measurable rates.

claim: AAAR-1.0 (Pang et al., 2024) is a benchmark of four peer-review-aligned tasks (equation inference, experiment design, paper weakness identification, review generation) where even GPT-4o scored below expert human reviewers on every sub-task.

claim: LLM-as-referee study (Bauwens & Smeesters, 2024) found, via a controlled experiment on 3,000 actual manuscript submissions to a marketing journal, that GPT-4 review scores correlated at r=0.41 with eventual editorial decisions, validating LLMs as a triage signal.

None of the candidates match this specific system or finding — the closest is openalex:W4411120114 which involves LLMs summarizing peer reviews, but that is a different task and method entirely.

claim: OpenScholar (Asai et al., 2024) released a 45-million-paper retrieval corpus and a specialised 7B model fine-tuned to synthesise literature; it outperformed GPT-4 with retrieval on citation-backed question answering, reducing unsupported claims by ~30% on ScholarQA-Bench.
reason: The claim describes OpenScholar (Asai et al., 2024) with a 45M-paper corpus, a 7B fine-tuned model, and ScholarQA-Bench results. None of the candidates match this specific system or benchmark — the candidates cover PaperQA2, LitQA2, KGCheck, FACT, and other RAG agents, but not OpenScholar.

reason: This candidate explicitly describes the AI Scientist as an end-to-end, fully automated pipeline that generates research ideas, runs experiments, writes manuscripts, and performs self-review at under $15 per paper — matching the claim's description of the Sakana AI system including open-ended loop and ML tasks.

claim: SciAgent (Wang et al., 2024): a tool-augmented LLM agent for scientific problem solving that retrieves and executes domain tools (calculators, databases, simulation APIs), evaluated on the SciToolBench benchmark spanning chemistry, physics, and biology.
reason: The claim describes SciAgent with SciToolBench as the evaluation benchmark. The closest candidates are arxiv:2602.12984v2::k00 (SciAgentGym, an environment) and arxiv:2602.12984v2::k01 (SciAgentBench, an evaluation suite) — these are related but neither matches SciToolBench. No candidate describes the specific SciAgent system evaluated on SciToolBench from Wang et al. 2024.

claim: MLAgentBench (Huang et al., 2024): a benchmark of 13 realistic ML research tasks (e.g., improving CIFAR-10 accuracy, debugging a transformer) used to evaluate autonomous coding and experimentation agents such as ReAct and Claude, revealing that agents still fail on multi-step experimental planning.

The closest candidate is MLGym-Bench (k01), which also has 13 AI research tasks, but these are distinct benchmarks from different papers with different task sets and evaluation scopes. No candidate matches the specific MLAgentBench system described in the claim.

claim: DiscoveryBench (Majumder et al., 2024): a benchmark of 264 real data-driven discovery tasks drawn from published social-science and life-science papers, measuring whether LLM agents can reproduce statistical findings from raw datasets—GPT-4o achieved only ~6% end-to-end success.

None describe the same DiscoveryBench system with 264 tasks and ~6% GPT-4o success rate.

claim: ResearchAgent (Baek et al., 2024): an academic-paper-writing agent that iteratively refines ideas, outlines, and full drafts using a retrieval-augmented knowledge graph of PubMed and Semantic Scholar papers, outperforming GPT-4 baselines on human-rated novelty and clarity.
reason: The claim describes ResearchAgent by Baek et al. (2024), which uses a knowledge graph from academic papers (PubMed/Semantic Scholar) to iteratively refine research outputs. Candidate k00 from W4411120082 describes the same system: ResearchAgent that augments papers with an academic-graph neighbourhood and entity-centric knowledge store, iteratively refining via ReviewingAgents — same system, same authors, same mechanism.

claim: SciCode (Tian et al., 2024): a coding benchmark constructed from 338 research-level subproblems across 80 scientific papers in mathematics, physics, and biology; frontier models including GPT-4o solved fewer than 5% of the hardest subproblems unaided.
reason: The claim describes SciCode, a specific benchmark of 338 subproblems from 80 scientific papers across math, physics, and biology, with a specific finding about GPT-4o solving <5% of hardest subproblems. None of the candidates describe SciCode or this specific benchmark/finding.

claim: OpenHands SWE-bench result (Wang et al., 2024): the CodeAct agent running inside the OpenHands framework achieved 26.7% resolve rate on SWE-bench Verified, establishing a reproducible open-source baseline for software-engineering agents and influencing how lab-automation coding agents are evaluated.

reason: The candidate describes an Intelligent Agent combining multiple LLMs to autonomously design, plan, and execute scientific experiments including catalyzed cross-coupling reactions in chemistry — this is the same Coscientist system described in the claim.

claim: LLM-SR (Shojaee et al., 2024): a method combining LLMs with evolutionary search for symbolic regression of scientific equations; outperformed state-of-the-art symbolic regression baselines (e.g., PySR) on physics and biology datasets by using LLMs to propose structurally meaningful equation skeletons.

claim: AgentReview (Jin et al., 2024): a simulation of the NeurIPS peer-review process using LLM agents as authors, reviewers, and area chairs; showed that LLM reviewers exhibit similar biases to humans (sycophancy, authority bias) and that multi-agent deliberation reduced score variance.
reason: The claim describes AgentReview, an LLM agent-based simulation of the NeurIPS peer-review process with authors, reviewers, and area chairs. claim: PaperBench (Starace et al., OpenAI, 2025): a benchmark where AI agents attempt to replicate 20 ICML 2024 papers from scratch, including coding and running experiments; Claude 3.5 Sonnet achieved the highest replication score (~21%) among evaluated models.

claim: HypoGeniC (Zhou et al., 2024): a hypothesis-generation framework that prompts LLMs to iteratively produce, critique, and rank scientific hypotheses grounded in retrieved literature; evaluated on biomedical and social-science corpora and shown to surface novel, literature-supported hypotheses faster than baseline retrieval methods.

This is the same system, despite the claim emphasizing biomedical/social-science corpora and literature retrieval while the candidate focuses on the algorithmic mechanism.

This matches arxiv:2510.24591v2::k01, which describes an evaluation framework for scientific AI agents measuring replication success on end-result-graded tasks assessing faithfulness and correctness. The '<15% success rate' detail is consistent with the paper's findings. The other candidates are about biomedical ML (BioML-bench), Kaggle tabular tasks, or social science reproducibility — different domains and systems.

claim: ResearchAgent (Baek et al., 2024) is an iterative framework in which one LLM generates paper ideas and another critiques them using retrieved literature, showing that iterative critic feedback raises human-rated idea quality scores by over 20% compared to single-pass generation.
reason: This candidate describes LLM-based ReviewingAgents that provide iterative feedback aligned with human preferences and measurably improve research idea quality — matching the claim's core contribution of a critic LLM improving idea quality scores through iterative feedback. The other candidates involve hypothesis generation in scientific domains (not paper ideas) or 3-agent workflows that differ structurally.

claim: AI Scientist (Lu et al., 2024, Sakana AI): an end-to-end autonomous research system that generates hypotheses, writes code, runs experiments, and produces a full manuscript with LaTeX; demonstrated on ML tasks including NLP and vision, completing a research cycle in under $15 per paper.
reason: Both the claim and this candidate describe the same AI Scientist system by Sakana AI (Lu et al., 2024): an end-to-end autonomous pipeline that generates ideas, runs experiments, writes a full manuscript, and completes a research cycle at under $15 per paper.

claim: MLAgentBench (Huang et al., 2024): a benchmark of 13 ML research tasks (e.g., Kaggle competitions, model improvement tasks) used to evaluate autonomous ML-engineering agents; baseline agents (Claude, GPT-4) solved only a subset, establishing a hard difficulty floor.

The closest candidate is openalex:W4407806895::k01 (MLGym-Bench), which also has 13 tasks and evaluates LLM agents on research workflows, but it is a different system from a different group. No candidate describes MLAgentBench specifically.

claim: SciAgent (2024): a multi-agent LLM framework for tool-augmented scientific problem-solving, evaluated on SciToolBench, showing that tool use substantially outperforms plain LLM reasoning on quantitative science tasks.

The name 'SciToolBench' in the claim is a paraphrase/slight variation of SciAgentBench; the overall framing (tool-augmented scientific problem-solving, benchmark evaluation) maps to this contribution.

claim: ResearchBench / SciCode (Tian et al., 2024): a benchmark of 338 real scientific coding problems drawn from papers in math, physics, and CS; GPT-4o achieves ~4.6% on the hardest subproblems, revealing a large gap between LLM coding ability and actual research-level implementation.

None of the candidates describe SWE-bench; they describe other benchmarks (EXP-Bench, PaperBench, MATDESIGN, a chemistry hypothesis benchmark, a scientific literature task benchmark, and a scientific coding task evaluation). The closest is openalex:W4403324055::k03 which evaluates agents on scientific coding tasks and references inference-time scaling, but it is a different system measuring agent performance, not the SWE-bench benchmark itself.

claim: Guo et al. (2024, 'DS-Agent', arXiv) introduced a data-science agent that autonomously retrieves relevant Kaggle solutions via case-based reasoning and adapts them, achieving state-of-the-art on automated data analysis benchmarks.
reason: The claim describes DS-Agent (Guo et al., 2024), a specific system using case-based reasoning to retrieve Kaggle solutions for automated data science tasks. None of the candidates match this specific system or contribution — they describe different systems (tree-search ML engineering, tool creation agents, hypothesis refinement, ReAct operators, LLM science benchmarks, WebThinker).

claim: Schmidgall et al. (2024, 'AgentClinic', arXiv) proposed a multimodal clinical-agent benchmark in which LLM agents must gather patient information and diagnose disease, enabling evaluation of scientific reasoning under interactive constraint.
reason: The claim describes AgentClinic, a multimodal benchmark where LLM agents interactively gather patient information and diagnose disease. None of the candidates match this — they cover peer review simulation, literature QA, materials discovery, web navigation, test-time scaling, and genetic disease QA. No candidate is the same system or benchmark.

claim: Bran et al. (2024, 'ChemCrow', Nature Machine Intelligence) demonstrated a chemistry-specialized LLM agent that integrates 18 expert tools to autonomously plan and reason about organic synthesis routes.
reason: The claim describes ChemCrow, a chemistry-specialized LLM agent integrating 18 expert tools for organic synthesis reasoning. None of the candidates match this specific system — the closest candidate (openalex:W4365211632::k00) describes a multi-LLM agent for scientific experiments including chemistry, but it is a different system (not ChemCrow) with a different architecture and scope.

claim: Jansen et al. (2024, 'DiscoveryBench', arXiv) introduced a benchmark of 264 real-world discovery tasks drawn from published studies, measuring whether agents can reproduce data-driven scientific conclusions from raw datasets.
reason: The claim describes DiscoveryBench, a benchmark of 264 real-world discovery tasks measuring whether agents can reproduce data-driven scientific conclusions from raw datasets. None of the candidates match this specific system — they describe different benchmarks (MATDESIGN, EXP-Bench, a reproducibility corpus of 76 studies, HypoBench, an AI-human comparison methodology, and a chemistry hypothesis benchmark), none of which is DiscoveryBench.

claim: Kambhampati et al. (2024, 'LLMs Can't Plan But Can Help Planning', arXiv) provided a critical analysis establishing that pure LLM agents fail on rigorous multi-step scientific planning without external verifiers, motivating hybrid neuro-symbolic agent architectures.
reason: The claim refers specifically to Kambhampati et al. (2024) 'LLMs Can't Plan But Can Help Planning', a paper arguing LLMs cannot autonomously plan but can assist symbolic planners. None of the candidates match this specific paper or contribution — the closest is openalex:W7118690722::k01 which notes a capability gap in AI research frameworks, but that is a different system/finding about AI research automation frameworks, not the Kambhampati neuro-symbolic planning argument.

claim: Boiko et al. (2023, Coscientist, Nature) demonstrated a fully autonomous LLM-based agent that designs, plans, and executes chemical synthesis experiments end-to-end, including operating robotic lab hardware.
reason: The claim describes Coscientist (Boiko et al., 2023, Nature), an LLM agent for autonomous chemical synthesis including robotic lab hardware operation. None of the candidates match this specific system — the candidates cover ML research automation pipelines and BioMARS (biological robotics), not chemical synthesis.

claim: Gottweis et al. (2025, AlphaScientist / Google DeepMind) introduced a self-driven AI scientist that generates novel hypotheses, designs experiments, and iterates on results across multiple scientific domains using tool-augmented LLMs.
reason: The claim refers to AlphaScientist by Gottweis et al. (2025, Google DeepMind), a specific system not present among the candidates. The two 'AI Scientist' entries are from Sakana AI, not Google DeepMind, and describe different systems. No candidate matches the same specific contribution.

claim: Lu et al. (2024, AI Scientist, Sakana AI) presented an end-to-end system in which LLM agents autonomously generate research ideas, run experiments, write papers, and perform self-evaluation in an iterative loop.
reason: The claim describes the original AI Scientist paper by Lu et al. (2024, Sakana AI) — an end-to-end LLM pipeline for autonomous idea generation, experiment execution, paper writing, and self-review. Candidate k00 from W4402952666 matches this exactly, including the cost figure and open-ended loop description.

claim: Liang et al. (2024, Can LLMs Generate Novel Research Ideas?) provided a large-scale human-evaluated benchmark showing that GPT-4-class models can produce research ideas rated as novel and feasible by domain experts, though execution quality lags.
reason: The claim describes Liang et al. (2024) with a large-scale human evaluation by domain experts finding LLM-generated ideas rated as novel but with execution quality lagging. This matches W4403586302::k01, which describes a blind evaluation by over 100 NLP researchers finding LLM-generated ideas rated significantly more novel than human expert ideas but slightly weaker on feasibility.

claim: Si et al. (2024, SciAgent) introduced a retrieval-augmented multi-agent framework that decomposes complex scientific questions into sub-tasks, retrieves relevant literature, and synthesizes grounded answers.
reason: The claim describes SciAgent by Si et al. (2024), a retrieval-augmented multi-agent framework for complex scientific question answering with sub-task decomposition and literature retrieval. No candidate matches this specific system: PaperQA (W4389761608) is a related RAG system but is a different paper/system; SciAgentBench (arxiv:2602.12984v2) is a benchmark, not the SciAgent framework itself; the other candidates are unrelated systems.

claim: Ajith et al. (2024, ReviewerGPT) systematically evaluated GPT-4 as an automated peer reviewer across NLP venues, revealing both promise in surface-level critique and limitations in detecting technical errors.
reason: The claim describes ReviewerGPT's finding that GPT-4 shows promise in surface-level critique but struggles with technical errors. Candidate k02 from the same paper (W4384261641) describes exactly this: GPT-4 reliably detects negated abstract claims (surface-level) but struggles with sentence-level informality errors, matching the empirical finding about limitations in detecting technical errors.

claim: Wang et al. (2024, SciPIP) proposed a structured pipeline in which LLMs iteratively refine research hypotheses by querying knowledge graphs and literature corpora, improving hypothesis novelty and verifiability.
reason: The claim describes SciPIP, a specific pipeline for iterative hypothesis refinement via knowledge graphs and literature corpora. While KG-CoI (W4404355537::k00) also uses knowledge graphs for hypothesis generation, it is a different system with different components (chain-of-idea generation, hallucination detection). None of the candidates match the specific SciPIP system and its iterative refinement mechanism.

claim: Guo et al. (2024, FrontierMath, Epoch AI) released a benchmark of expert-level mathematical problems designed to be unsolvable by current LLMs, establishing a frontier for evaluating quantitative reasoning in scientific AI.
reason: The claim refers to FrontierMath, a specific benchmark of expert-level mathematical problems by Epoch AI. None of the candidates describe FrontierMath or a mathematics-focused benchmark; they cover ML research replication, hypothesis generation, paper review quality, frontier scientific questions, AI-generated papers, and limitation identification.

claim: Jansen et al. (2024, DiscoveryBench) constructed a multi-domain scientific-discovery benchmark requiring agents to perform hypothesis generation, data analysis, and conclusion synthesis from raw datasets.
reason: The claim describes DiscoveryBench by Jansen et al. (2024), a multi-domain benchmark for scientific discovery covering hypothesis generation, data analysis, and conclusion synthesis from raw datasets. None of the candidates match this specific benchmark — HypoBench, MATDESIGN, EXP-Bench, CoDaS, and the knowledge-graph masking benchmark are all distinct systems with different designs and scopes.

claim: Swanson et al. (2024, EntiGraph / knowledge graph agents) showed that LLM agents can construct dense entity-relation graphs from scientific corpora and use them to answer complex cross-paper queries with higher accuracy than RAG baselines.
reason: The claim describes EntiGraph, a specific system that constructs entity-relation graphs from scientific corpora for cross-paper QA. None of the candidates match this: PaperQA is an agent-based RAG system without knowledge graph construction; ResearchAgent uses an entity-centric knowledge store but for research ideation, not cross-paper QA via KGs; the others address biomedical KG benchmarking, causal graphs for hypothesis generation, or subgraph sampling. No candidate describes the same specific contribution.

claim: Huang et al. (2024, MLAgentBench) proposed a benchmark for evaluating LLM agents on end-to-end machine learning research tasks—data processing, model selection, and experiment scripting—providing a reproducible testbed.
reason: The claim describes MLAgentBench by Huang et al. (2024), a specific benchmark for evaluating LLM agents on end-to-end ML research tasks. None of the candidates match this specific system. The closest candidates (MLGym-Bench, MLR-Bench, Weco Kaggle Benchmark) are different benchmarks with different designs, task sets, and authors.

claim: Schmidgall et al. (2024, AgentLaboratory) released an open framework for multi-agent collaborative scientific research in which specialized sub-agents handle literature review, experimentation, and manuscript writing.
reason: Agent Laboratory matches the claim exactly: it is the Schmidgall et al. AgentLaboratory system — an autonomous LLM pipeline with specialized sub-agents covering literature review, experimentation, and report/manuscript writing, with optional human feedback.

claim: Zhang et al. (2025, ResearchAgent) demonstrated a feedback-driven agentic loop in which reviewer-like critic agents iteratively improve research proposals generated by proposer agents, raising human-judged quality scores significantly.
reason: The claim describes ResearchAgent, a system where reviewer-like critic agents (ReviewingAgents) iteratively improve research proposals generated by proposer agents — this is exactly what openalex:W4411120082::k00 describes.

claim: OpenScholar (Asai et al., 2024) introduced a retrieval-and-synthesis pipeline grounded in a large open-access scientific corpus that outperforms GPT-4 on literature-grounded question answering while citing sources faithfully.
reason: The claim describes OpenScholar (Asai et al., 2024), a specific retrieval-and-synthesis system grounded in a large open-access scientific corpus. None of the candidates match this system. The closest candidate is PaperQA (openalex:W4389761608::k00), which is also a RAG system for scientific literature, but it is a different system with a different architecture (agent-based, tool-decomposed) and different identity. OpenScholar is not among the candidates.

claim: Chan et al. (2024, MLAB / automated lab agents) developed a modular LLM-agent architecture that interfaces with laboratory information management systems and instruments to automate experimental workflows in biology and chemistry.
reason: The claim describes MLAB, a specific modular LLM-agent system interfacing with laboratory information management systems (LIMS) and instruments for biology/chemistry automation. None of the candidates match this: AILA is AFM-specific, W4411721350 focuses on robotic liquid handling and metabolomics for hypothesis validation, and KBase Research Agent targets genome assembly. No candidate describes a LIMS-integrated, general-purpose lab automation agent named or matching MLAB.

claim: Boiko et al. (2023, 'Emergent autonomous scientific research capabilities of large language models') demonstrated that a GPT-4-based agent named Coscientist could autonomously plan and execute multi-step chemical experiments including literature search, code writing, and robotic control.
reason: This candidate describes an intelligent agent system using multiple LLMs to autonomously design, plan, and execute scientific experiments including catalyzed cross-coupling reactions in chemistry — matching Coscientist's specific demonstrated capabilities (literature search, code writing, robotic control for chemical experiments including cross-coupling reactions) from Boiko et al. 2023.

claim: Gottweis et al. (2025, 'Towards an AI co-scientist') introduced a multi-agent system built on Gemini that generates, critiques, and ranks novel scientific hypotheses, showing performance competitive with domain experts on biomedical tasks.
reason: The claim describes Gottweis et al.'s AI co-scientist, a multi-agent system on Gemini that generates, critiques, and ranks hypotheses for biomedical tasks. Candidate k00 matches exactly: it is the AI co-scientist multi-agent system built on Gemini 2.0 with specialized agents for generation, reflection, ranking, and evolution of research hypotheses.

claim: Lu et al. (2024, 'The AI Scientist') presented a fully automated LLM pipeline that conceives, implements, and writes up machine-learning research papers, generating peer-reviewable manuscripts end-to-end without human input.
reason: The claim describes 'The AI Scientist' by Lu et al. (2024) as a fully automated LLM pipeline that conceives, implements, and writes up ML research papers end-to-end. Candidate W4402952666::k00 matches this exactly: it names 'The AI Scientist', describes it as an end-to-end fully automated pipeline where a frontier LLM generates ideas, executes experiments, writes manuscripts, and performs self-review, at under $15 per paper — the same 2024 paper.

claim: Liang et al. (2024, 'Can LLMs Give Constructive Feedback?') benchmarked GPT-4 as an automated peer reviewer against human reviews on NeurIPS submissions, finding moderate alignment but systematic weaknesses in detecting methodological flaws.
reason: The claim describes a specific benchmark study by Liang et al. (2024) comparing GPT-4 peer reviews to human reviews on NeurIPS submissions with findings about moderate alignment and weaknesses in detecting methodological flaws. None of the candidates match this specific study — the closest candidate (openalex:W4384261641::k00) involves helpfulness ratings comparison but is a different paper with different findings and framing.

claim: Si et al. (2024, 'Can LLMs Generate Novel Research Ideas?') ran a controlled study showing that GPT-4-generated research ideas were rated as more novel than expert human ideas, though the ideas were less feasible on average.
reason: The claim describes the same specific finding: a controlled blind study by 100+ NLP researchers where LLM-generated ideas were rated significantly more novel than human expert ideas but weaker on feasibility — matching k01 from W4403586302 exactly.

claim: Wang et al. (2024, 'SciCode') released a benchmark of 338 research-level coding problems drawn from published scientific papers to evaluate whether LLMs can solve authentic scientific programming tasks.
reason: The claim describes SciCode, a benchmark of 338 research-level coding problems from scientific papers for evaluating LLMs on scientific programming tasks. None of the candidates match this specific benchmark — they describe different benchmarks (chemistry hypothesis discovery, AI research experiments, scientific discovery evaluation, algorithm optimization, and general scientific discovery), none of which is SciCode.

claim: Jansen et al. (2024, 'DiscoveryBench') proposed a benchmark requiring agents to reproduce discovery workflows from social- and natural-science datasets by identifying variables, forming hypotheses, and testing them with code.
reason: DiscoveryBench is a benchmark for autonomous data-driven discovery from social- and natural-science datasets, requiring agents to identify variables, form hypotheses, and test them with code. None of the candidates match this specific system. The closest candidates (CORE-Bench, REPRO-Bench) focus on computational reproducibility of existing papers, not discovery workflows from raw datasets.

claim: Swanson et al. (2024, 'Virtual Lab') described a multi-agent LLM framework that simulates a scientific team with specialized roles—PI, domain expert, critic—to iteratively design nanobody binders validated by wet-lab experiments.
reason: The claim describes the Virtual Lab system (Swanson et al. 2024) — a multi-agent LLM framework with PI, domain expert, and critic roles for iterative nanobody binder design validated by wet-lab experiments. None of the candidates match this specific system. The closest thematically are SciAgents and Robin, but they are different systems with different architectures, roles, and scientific domains. No candidate describes Virtual Lab or nanobody design.

claim: Ghafarollahi & Buehler (2024, 'SciAgents') built a graph-RAG agent system over large scientific corpora that autonomously traverses knowledge graphs to generate and test multi-hop mechanistic hypotheses in materials science.
reason: The claim describes SciAgents by Ghafarollahi & Buehler, a graph-RAG multi-agent system over scientific knowledge graphs for hypothesis generation — this matches candidate k00 which is the SciAgents multi-agent framework integrating ontological knowledge graphs with LLM-based agents for scientific discovery.

claim: OpenScholar (Asai et al., 2024) introduced a retrieval-augmented LLM tailored for scientific literature synthesis, combining a 45M-paper datastore with a self-feedback pipeline that substantially reduces hallucinated citations.
reason: The claim describes OpenScholar (Asai et al., 2024), a specific system with a 45M-paper datastore and self-feedback pipeline for scientific literature synthesis. None of the candidates match this specific system — PaperQA is a different RAG system, and the others are unrelated systems (review pipelines, RL frameworks, biomedical agents, aspect-based retrieval).

claim: Baek et al. (2024, 'ResearchAgent') proposed an iterative LLM agent that generates research ideas by first retrieving related papers via academic knowledge graphs and then refining ideas through simulated reviewer feedback.
reason: The claim describes ResearchAgent by Baek et al. (2024), which uses an LLM agent to generate research ideas by retrieving related papers via academic knowledge graphs and refining ideas through simulated reviewer feedback. Candidate k00 from the same paper (W4411120082) describes exactly this system: an LLM-powered ResearchAgent that augments a core paper with an academic-graph neighbourhood and iteratively refines output via ReviewingAgents feedback.

claim: Skarlinski et al. (2024, 'Language agents achieve superhuman synthesis of scientific knowledge') showed that a paper-QA agent with tool access to full-text PDFs surpassed domain expert performance on challenging biology question-answering.
reason: The claim describes PaperQA surpassing domain expert performance on scientific QA, which maps directly to k03's finding that a retrieval-augmented agent for scientific QA matches (and in the paper, surpasses) expert human researchers in accuracy on a full-text retrieval benchmark — the same system, same empirical finding, same paper (Skarlinski et al. 2024, W4389761608).

claim: Schmidgall et al. (2025, 'AgentClinic') presented a multimodal benchmark of simulated clinical research scenarios to evaluate medical AI agents on hypothesis-driven diagnostic reasoning with patient interaction loops.
reason: AgentClinic is a multimodal benchmark for evaluating medical AI agents in simulated clinical environments with patient interaction loops. None of the candidates describe this system — they describe Med-AI Bench (171 cases, 19 clinical tasks), Medical AI Scientist (end-to-end agentic research framework), SDE (scientific discovery evaluation), or other unrelated contributions.

claim: Mankowitz et al. (2023, 'Faster sorting algorithms discovered using deep reinforcement learning') established a precedent for AI-driven algorithmic discovery by training an RL agent that found sorting routines faster than any known human-designed algorithm, influencing subsequent autonomous research agent design.
reason: The claim refers specifically to Mankowitz et al. (2023) using deep RL to discover faster sorting algorithms — a specific algorithmic discovery result. None of the candidates describe this system or finding; they describe LLM agent frameworks, scientific review tools, contradiction detection, and ML engineering agents.

claim: Boiko et al. (2023, Coscientist) demonstrated a GPT-4-based autonomous agent that plans and executes multi-step chemistry experiments end-to-end, including robotic lab control, marking the first fully autonomous chemical synthesis agent.
reason: The claim describes Coscientist (Boiko et al. 2023), a specific GPT-4-based autonomous chemistry agent with robotic lab control. None of the candidates describe Coscientist — they describe BioMARS, FalsificationAgent, a multi-agent LLM framework for X-ray/materials science, Robin, The AI Scientist, and Orion. All are distinct systems from different papers.

claim: Jumper et al. / the AlphaFold3 team (2024) extended structure prediction to biomolecular complexes (proteins, DNA, RNA, ligands), enabling AI-driven hypothesis generation about molecular interactions at proteome scale.
reason: The claim describes AlphaFold3's extension to biomolecular complex structure prediction (proteins, DNA, RNA, ligands). None of the candidates describe this specific system or contribution — they cover AI hypothesis generation frameworks, multi-agent discussion mechanisms, contradiction detection, and causal hypothesis generation methods, none of which is AlphaFold3 or biomolecular structure prediction.

claim: Lu et al. (2024, AI Scientist) introduced a closed-loop system in which LLM agents autonomously generate research ideas, write code, run experiments, analyze results, and produce manuscript drafts across multiple ML sub-fields.
reason: The claim describes the AI Scientist by Lu et al. (2024) as a closed-loop system where LLM agents autonomously generate ideas, write code, run experiments, analyze results, and produce manuscripts across ML sub-fields. This matches W7140287209::k00 exactly, which describes the same system with the same capabilities. W4402952666::k00 also describes the AI Scientist but adds the $15/paper cost detail and 'self-review' framing; W7140287209::k00 is the closer match to the 2024 paper's description without v2 additions.

claim: Wang et al. (2024, SciAgent) proposed a multi-agent framework with specialized roles—literature agent, hypothesis agent, experiment agent—that collaboratively accelerates the full scientific discovery loop in drug-target interaction tasks.
reason: The claim describes 'SciAgent' by Wang et al. (2024) focused specifically on drug-target interaction tasks with roles named literature agent, hypothesis agent, and experiment agent. The closest candidate is openalex:W4405599065::k00 ('SciAgents'), but that system uses ontological knowledge graphs and is not specific to drug-target interaction tasks. None of the candidates match the specific system, domain focus, and agent role names described in the claim.

claim: Gottweis et al. (2025, AlphaFold for Science) showed that large language models can serve as virtual scientific collaborators by autonomously proposing and refining hypotheses grounded in structured biological databases.
reason: The claim references Gottweis et al. (2025) describing LLMs as 'virtual scientific collaborators' that autonomously propose and refine hypotheses grounded in structured biological databases. None of the candidates match this specific system or paper — the closest candidates describe autonomous experiment execution (BioMARS, Intelligent Agent) or hypothesis generation broadly, but none describe the specific Gottweis et al. system framed as a virtual collaborator.

claim: Huang et al. (2024, 'MLAgentBench') introduced an evaluation suite in which agents must autonomously conduct machine-learning experiments—running baselines, iterating on models, and improving metrics—measured against human ML engineer performance.
reason: MLAgentBench (Huang et al. 2024) is a specific benchmark where agents autonomously conduct ML experiments and are measured against human ML engineer performance. The closest candidate is RE-Bench (arxiv:2411.15114v2::k00), which also benchmarks AI agents on ML research engineering tasks against human experts, but RE-Bench is a different system with different tasks and framing. None of the candidates describe MLAgentBench specifically.

claim: Liang et al. (2024) released the MLAgentBench benchmark, providing a suite of ML research tasks (dataset exploration, model improvement, Kaggle-style competitions) to evaluate autonomous ML-research agents.
reason: MLAgentBench (Liang et al., 2024) is a specific benchmark for evaluating autonomous ML-research agents with tasks including dataset exploration, model improvement, and Kaggle-style competitions. None of the candidates describe this same system — the candidates describe Weco Kaggle Benchmark, MLRC-Bench, MLGym-Bench, and other distinct systems/findings.

claim: Huang et al. (2024, MLR-Copilot) built an agent that reads ML papers, extracts experimental protocols, and reproduces results automatically, establishing a reproducibility-focused evaluation paradigm.
reason: The claim describes MLR-Copilot by Huang et al. (2024), a system that reads ML papers and reproduces results. The candidate k00 is explicitly named MLR-Copilot and describes the same three-stage framework taking research papers as input to produce results.

claim: Si et al. (2024) conducted a large-scale human study showing that LLM-generated research ideas were rated as more novel than human-generated ideas by domain experts, while also being less feasible, quantifying the creativity-feasibility trade-off.
reason: The claim describes a large-scale human study with 100+ NLP researchers finding LLM ideas more novel but less feasible than human ideas — this matches k01 from W4403586302 which describes exactly this blind evaluation with the same statistical finding and the same novelty/feasibility trade-off.

claim: Gao et al. (2024, AgentReview) demonstrated that multi-agent LLM panels can simulate the peer-review process, revealing systematic biases (authority bias, positivity bias) comparable to those observed in human reviewers.
reason: The claim describes AgentReview (Gao et al., 2024), a multi-agent LLM framework that simulates the full peer-review process and uncovers biases like authority bias and positivity bias comparable to human reviewers. None of the candidates match this specific contribution: k03 and k04 describe vulnerability analyses of LLM reviewers (prestige/authorship bias, hallucination), not a multi-agent simulation framework; k00/GAR focuses on automating review feedback and acceptance prediction; k01 addresses author self-disclosure manipulation; and k02 covers meta-review pipeline biases. The AgentReview simulation-and-bias-comparison framing is absent from all candidates.

claim: d'Arcy et al. (2024, MARG) proposed a multi-agent argumentation framework for AI-assisted review that decomposes reviewer disagreement into structured arguments, improving review quality over single-agent baselines.
reason: The claim refers to MARG by d'Arcy et al., which is the same system described in k00 (Multi-Agent Review Generation), where multiple LLM instances engage in structured internal discussion to collectively generate peer-review feedback. The claim paraphrases the structured multi-agent discussion as 'decomposing reviewer disagreement into structured arguments', which corresponds to the same contribution despite slight misdescription of the mechanism.

claim: Roohani et al. (2024, BioDiscoveryAgent) developed an agent that interprets CRISPR screen results and proposes follow-up gene perturbation experiments, closing the loop between experimental data and next-step hypothesis generation.
reason: The claim describes BioDiscoveryAgent, a specific agent that interprets CRISPR screen results and proposes follow-up gene perturbation experiments. None of the candidates match this — they describe CRISPR-GPT (experiment design), Robin (multi-agent scientific discovery), a gene-editing benchmark, adversarial hypothesis generation, MOOSE-Chem, or validated gene-editing workflows. None is BioDiscoveryAgent or matches its specific contribution of closing the loop between CRISPR screen data and next-step gene perturbation proposals.

claim: Ma et al. (2024, DrugAgent) showed that an LLM agent equipped with biochemistry tool-use (docking simulators, ADMET predictors) can autonomously prioritize drug candidates, outperforming single-model baselines on virtual screening benchmarks.
reason: The claim describes DrugAgent (Ma et al., 2024), a specific LLM agent using biochemistry tools (docking simulators, ADMET predictors) for drug candidate prioritization on virtual screening benchmarks. None of the candidates describe this specific system or benchmark. The closest candidate (W4416043407::k03) is about AI-generated drug repurposing for AML validated in vitro — a different contribution with no mention of DrugAgent, docking simulators, ADMET predictors, or virtual screening benchmarks.

claim: Gu et al. (2025, ScienceAgentBench) introduced a benchmark of 102 real scientific coding tasks spanning five domains to evaluate how well LLM agents translate research objectives into executable data-analysis workflows.
reason: ScienceAgentBench (Gu et al. 2025) is a benchmark of 102 real scientific coding tasks for evaluating LLM agents on data-analysis workflows. None of the candidates match: the closest are GeneBench (103 problems in genomics/biology) and BioMed-AQA (327 biomedical tasks), but these are different systems with different scopes, domains, and authors. No candidate describes ScienceAgentBench specifically.

claim: Ifargan et al. (2025) presented an autonomous LLM pipeline for statistical data analysis that plans analyses, writes and debugs code, and interprets results, achieving performance comparable to expert human analysts on tabular biomedical datasets.
reason: The claim describes Ifargan et al. (2025)'s autonomous pipeline specifically for statistical data analysis on tabular biomedical datasets, benchmarked against expert human analysts. None of the candidates match this: BioMedAgent (k00) focuses on bioinformatics workflows broadly; the reproducibility pipeline (arxiv:2606.13670::k00) targets reproducing published statistical claims, not general analysis; AI Scientist targets ML research; BioMed-AQA is a benchmark; ExperimentAgent translates experiment plans to code; and the multi-agent evaluation (k03) covers scRNA-seq and metadata reconstruction tasks. No candidate describes the same system or empirical finding.

claim: Li et al. (2024, LitLLM / ResearchAgent) built a retrieval-augmented generation system for literature surveying that iteratively queries knowledge graphs and citation networks to produce coherent, grounded related-work sections for scientific papers.
reason: None of the candidates describe LitLLM or ResearchAgent — a RAG system specifically for iteratively querying knowledge graphs and citation networks to produce related-work sections. The candidates cover KG-CoI (hypothesis generation), SciMuse (collaboration ideas), PaperQA2 (Q&A agent), and other unrelated systems.

claim: Agent Laboratory (2025, arXiv:2501.04227) introduced a multi-agent framework where LLM agents act as research assistants across the full research lifecycle (literature review, experimentation, manuscript preparation), achieving near-human-quality paper outputs with minimal human intervention.
reason: The claim describes Agent Laboratory (arXiv:2501.04227), an autonomous LLM pipeline covering literature review, experimentation, and manuscript preparation with optional human feedback. The candidate openalex:W4416035220::k00 explicitly names 'Agent Laboratory' and describes the same system with the same scope.

claim: AI Scientist-v2 (Sakana AI, 2025) extended the original system via agentic tree search to produce workshop-level manuscripts, with one fully AI-generated paper achieving scores exceeding the average human acceptance threshold when submitted to an ICLR workshop — the first documented case of a fully autonomous paper passing peer review.
reason: The claim specifically describes a fully AI-generated paper exceeding the average human acceptance threshold at an ICLR workshop — the same specific empirical finding as k01, which states exactly that a fully AI-generated manuscript passed peer review at an ICLR workshop exceeding the average human acceptance threshold.

claim: The AI Scientist (Sakana AI, 2024) demonstrated the first end-to-end autonomous system capable of generating research ideas, implementing experiments, writing papers, and conducting simulated peer review, establishing a full-pipeline benchmark for autonomous scientific discovery.
reason: The claim describes the AI Scientist (Sakana AI, 2024) as an end-to-end autonomous system for generating ideas, running experiments, writing papers, and performing peer review at low cost — this matches W4402952666::k00 which describes the same system with the same capabilities including the under $15 per paper cost detail.

claim: DiscoveryBench (Majumder et al., 2024) established a benchmark for data-driven automated discovery, evaluating LLMs on their ability to generate high-specificity, verifiable hypotheses from structured datasets, revealing systematic gaps in current frontier models.
reason: The claim describes DiscoveryBench, a benchmark for data-driven automated discovery evaluating LLMs on generating verifiable hypotheses from structured datasets, revealing gaps in frontier models. Candidate k03 matches this: it describes controlled synthetic datasets with programmatically varied difficulty revealing that hypothesis discovery rates collapse as complexity grows, quantifying capability gaps — this is the same empirical finding from DiscoveryBench (Majumder et al., 2024).

claim: ResearchBench (Liu et al., 2025, arXiv:2503.21248) introduced a large-scale benchmark decomposing scientific discovery into inspiration retrieval, hypothesis composition, and hypothesis ranking across 12 disciplines, enabling standardized LLM evaluation on core research sub-tasks.
reason: The claim describes ResearchBench (arXiv:2503.21248), a large-scale multi-disciplinary benchmark spanning 12 disciplines. The candidates from W4403345771 share the same three-subtask decomposition (inspiration retrieval, hypothesis composition, ranking) but are chemistry-specific (MOOSE-Chem / 51 chemistry papers), not a 12-discipline benchmark. No candidate matches the specific ResearchBench system described in the claim.

claim: InternAgent (2025, arXiv:2505.16938) introduced a closed-loop hypothesis-to-verification system in which an LLM agent autonomously proposes hypotheses, designs experiments, executes them, and iteratively refines theories — demonstrating end-to-end scientific reasoning without human steering.
reason: InternAgent (arXiv:2505.16938) is a specific system not described by any candidate. The closest candidates are AI Scientist variants and ResearchAgent, but none match the specific closed-loop hypothesis-to-verification system named InternAgent.

claim: ResearchGym (2026, arXiv:2602.15112) provided a benchmark evaluating LLM agents on real-world AI research tasks, measuring end-to-end research capability beyond single-step generation and establishing reproducible evaluation protocols.
