None of the candidates describe this workshop itself; the candidates describe systems (Robin, AI Scientist-v2), benchmarks (ScholarEval, ForeSci), or task definitions.

claim: SciResearcher (2025) is a deep research agent scaled for frontier scientific reasoning tasks, combining retrieval-augmented generation with iterative reasoning to tackle multi-step scientific questions.
reason: The claim describes 'SciResearcher (2025)', a specific named deep research agent for scientific reasoning combining RAG and iterative reasoning. None of the candidates match this specific system. The closest candidate is arxiv:2504.21776::k00 (WebThinker), which is a deep research agent combining reasoning and web search, but it is a different named system with different specifics. No candidate represents SciResearcher.

claim: AI agents for energy materials research (2025) were demonstrated to orchestrate both literature review and laboratory execution—compressing tasks previously requiring months into days—using LLMs coupled with robotic lab hardware for autonomous synthesis and characterization.
reason: No candidate describes the same specific system. The claim describes an AI agent for energy materials research that integrates literature review with robotic laboratory execution, compressing months of work into days. The closest candidate (openalex:W7134059863::k00) involves a multi-agent LLM framework at scientific user facilities with robotic hardware but is not specific to energy materials research and does not mention the literature-review-to-lab-execution integration or the months-to-days compression framing. None of the candidates match the specific system described.

claim: IdeaBench (2025) benchmarks LLMs specifically for research idea generation quality, providing human-rated evaluations of novelty, feasibility, and significance to measure progress toward automated hypothesis proposal.
reason: The claim describes IdeaBench as a benchmark evaluating LLMs on research idea generation via human ratings of novelty, feasibility, and significance. Candidate W4416035327::k03 describes exactly this: human evaluation of 660 LLM-generated research ideas across novelty, relevance, and feasibility dimensions, matching the same system and evaluation approach.

claim: AI Scientist-v2 (Sakana AI, 2025) extended the original system with agentic tree search, enabling workshop-level automated scientific discovery and improving the quality and diversity of generated research ideas over the v1 system.
reason: The claim describes AI Scientist-v2 using agentic tree search for automated scientific discovery, which matches candidate k00's description of The AI Scientist-v2 using a progressive agentic tree-search methodology managed by a dedicated experiment manager agent.

claim: AI Scientist (Sakana AI, Lu et al., 2024) demonstrated the first end-to-end automated research pipeline covering idea generation, code implementation, experimentation, paper writing, and automated peer review, published as a preprint and widely cited as a proof-of-concept for fully autonomous research agents.
reason: The claim describes the AI Scientist by Sakana AI (Lu et al., 2024) as an end-to-end automated pipeline covering idea generation, code implementation, experimentation, paper writing, and automated peer review. Candidate openalex:W4402952666::k00 describes exactly this system with matching details including the frontier LLM generating ideas, executing experiments, writing manuscripts, performing self-review, and the specific cost figure of under $15 per paper. Both openalex:W7140287209::k00 and W4402952666::k00 describe the AI Scientist, but W4402952666::k00 is a more complete and specific match including the cost detail and open-ended loop description.

claim: ScienceAgentBench (2024-2025) benchmarks LLM agents on scientific data-analysis programming tasks drawn from real published research, testing the ability to write executable code that reproduces scientific results across disciplines.
reason: The claim describes ScienceAgentBench, a benchmark for LLM agents on scientific data-analysis programming tasks drawn from real published research across disciplines. This matches exactly the candidate describing ScienceAgentBench — 102 code-generation tasks from 44 peer-reviewed publications across four scientific disciplines, with self-contained Python programs as outputs.

claim: PaperBench (OpenAI, 2025) is a benchmark of 20 ICML Spotlight/Oral papers decomposed into 8,316 fine-grained sub-tasks; the best-performing agent scored only ~21%, establishing a concrete performance ceiling and exposing gaps in replication ability.
reason: The claim describes PaperBench's key empirical finding — frontier agents scoring at most ~21% on replication, establishing a performance ceiling — which directly matches k03. The benchmark description (20 ICML papers, 8,316 sub-tasks) from k00 is part of the same paper and consistent, but k03 captures the specific quantitative contribution the claim emphasizes: the 21% ceiling and the gap vs. human capability.

claim: CORE-Bench is a computational reproducibility benchmark for agents: given a published paper and its code, agents must reproduce reported numerical results, providing a reproducibility-focused evaluation distinct from idea-generation tasks.
reason: The claim describes CORE-Bench, a benchmark for AI agents on computational reproducibility using published papers and code, with multiple difficulty levels — this matches the candidate statement exactly.

claim: ChemCrow (Bran et al., Nature Machine Intelligence 2024) augments an LLM with 18 chemistry tools—including RDKit, PubChem, retrosynthesis engines, and robotic synthesis interfaces—enabling agents to autonomously plan and execute multi-step chemistry workflows.
reason: ChemCrow is a specific system that augments a single LLM with 18 chemistry tools (RDKit, PubChem, retrosynthesis engines, robotic synthesis interfaces). None of the candidates describe this specific system. The candidates describe different systems: a multi-LLM agent for scientific experiments, type-safe quantum chemistry agents, biomedical multi-agent frameworks, a robotic biology platform, a hypothesis-generation framework, and a multi-agent framework for scientific facilities. None is ChemCrow specifically.

claim: Coscientist (Boiko et al., Nature 2023/2024) showed GPT-4 can autonomously design, plan, and physically perform chemistry experiments by integrating web search, code execution, and robotic laboratory hardware control.
reason: This candidate describes an Intelligent Agent system using multiple LLMs to autonomously design, plan, and execute scientific experiments including catalyzed cross-coupling reactions in chemistry — matching Coscientist's demonstration of GPT-4-based autonomous chemistry experimentation with web search, code execution, and robotic hardware integration.

claim: A Nature-reported autonomous synthesis system (2024) completed the robotic synthesis of 29 organosilicon compounds, 8 of which were previously unknown, representing one of the first peer-reviewed demonstrations of AI-directed discovery of genuinely novel chemical entities.
reason: The claim describes a specific Nature-reported autonomous robotic synthesis system that produced 29 organosilicon compounds (8 novel) in 2024. None of the candidates match this: GALILEO focuses on peptide synthesis, Robin is a multi-agent literature/hypothesis system, and the others cover QA synthesis, peer review, benchmarks, or coding reproducibility. No candidate describes an organosilicon robotic synthesis system.

claim: Robin (FutureHouse, 2025) is an end-to-end drug repurposing agent that completed literature-to-experiment-design pipelines for 10 diseases in approximately 10 weeks, identifying candidate compounds for dry age-related macular degeneration (dry AMD) as a concrete output.
reason: The claim describes Robin, an end-to-end multi-agent system for autonomous scientific discovery including drug repurposing, which matches k00 describing Robin as a multi-agent system automating hypothesis generation and experimental design in a lab-in-the-loop framework. The mention of dry AMD as a concrete output also aligns with k01 and k02, but the claim's primary contribution being described is the Robin system itself (k00).

claim: FutureHouse's Crow, Falcon, and Owl agents (platform launched 2025) achieved ~90% accuracy on the LitQA literature-retrieval benchmark, outperforming PhD-level human biologists who scored ~67%, establishing a new state-of-the-art for scientific literature agents.
reason: The claim describes PaperQA2 (FutureHouse's platform with Crow, Falcon, Owl agents) achieving ~90% on LitQA and outperforming PhD-level humans (~67%), which matches the contribution about PaperQA2 matching or exceeding PhD-level expert performance on scientific literature retrieval tasks including LitQA.

claim: AgentReview (EMNLP 2024) studied peer review dynamics by having LLM agents play reviewer, area chair, and author roles in a simulated NeurIPS review process, finding that LLM reviewers exhibit systematic biases including susceptibility to authority and positivity bias.
reason: The claim specifically describes LLM reviewer biases including susceptibility to authority and positivity bias in the AgentReview NeurIPS simulation. Candidate k02 from the same paper (W4404783497) describes precisely these bias findings with quantified mechanisms including authority/halo effects and social conformity, matching the claim's specific empirical findings.

claim: AAAR-1.0 (2024-2025) is a benchmark evaluating agents on experiment design: given a research question and prior literature, agents must formulate hypotheses and produce rigorous experimental protocols, with frontier LLMs still far below expert human scores.
reason: AAAR-1.0 is a specific benchmark for experiment design (hypothesis formulation + experimental protocols), with frontier LLMs scoring well below human experts. None of the candidates describe this benchmark. The closest is PaperBench (arxiv:2504.01848v3::k03), which also shows a gap between AI and human experts, but it evaluates ML research replication, not experiment design from a research question. The other candidates describe different systems or benchmarks entirely.

claim: MLEbench (presented at ICLR 2025) evaluates ML agents on real machine-learning engineering tasks derived from Kaggle competitions, measuring end-to-end model-building ability and finding that even top agents solve fewer than 17% of tasks at human-competitive performance.
reason: MLEbench evaluates ML agents on Kaggle competition tasks specifically. The Weco Kaggle Benchmark (W4407760093::k02) is also Kaggle-based but describes a different system (Weco's benchmark, tabular tasks only, no mention of the <17% finding or ICLR 2025). The other candidates cover different benchmarks entirely. None is the same specific system as MLEbench.

claim: MLReplicate (2025, arXiv:2605.16616) benchmarks autonomous research systems specifically on machine-learning reproducibility, requiring agents to re-implement and reproduce results from ML papers end-to-end without human intervention.
reason: The claim describes MLReplicate, a benchmark specifically focused on ML reproducibility — requiring agents to re-implement and reproduce results from existing ML papers. The closest candidate, MLR-Bench (W4414587939::k00), evaluates agents on open-ended novel ML research tasks drawn from workshop papers, not on reproducing prior results. No candidate matches the specific reproducibility-focused benchmark described in the claim.

claim: The AI Scientist (Sakana AI, Lu et al., 2024) demonstrated the first end-to-end automated research system that generates hypotheses, runs experiments, writes manuscripts, and performs automated peer review across ML sub-fields, producing workshop-quality papers at roughly $15 per paper.
reason: This candidate explicitly names 'The AI Scientist', describes the same end-to-end automated pipeline (hypothesis generation, experiments, manuscript writing, peer review), and includes the specific '$15 per paper' cost figure mentioned in the claim.

claim: ResearchBench (2025) introduced an inspiration-based task decomposition protocol for evaluating LLMs on scientific discovery, breaking the discovery process into retrieving prior inspiration papers, identifying key concepts, and synthesizing novel hypotheses, providing fine-grained diagnostic scores across each sub-step.
reason: The claim describes ResearchBench (2025), a benchmark for evaluating LLMs on scientific discovery with fine-grained diagnostic scores across sub-steps (inspiration retrieval, key concept identification, hypothesis synthesis). No candidate matches this: k00 is about MOOSE-Chem's mathematical decomposition into subtasks (retrieving/composing/ranking), not a benchmark called ResearchBench. The other candidates are unrelated systems or frameworks.

claim: The AI Scientist-v2 (2025) extended the original system with an agentic tree-search guided by an experiment manager agent, removed reliance on human-authored templates, and achieved workshop-level submissions that passed automated review in multiple ML domains.
reason: The claim describes AI Scientist-v2 with agentic tree-search, experiment manager agent, no human-authored templates, and workshop-level ML submissions — matching the candidate statement exactly.

claim: SciCode (2024) introduced a research coding benchmark curated by domain scientists covering physics, chemistry, and biology, structured as hierarchical sub-problems to rigorously evaluate research-level programming skills of LLMs; frontier models solved fewer than 5% of problems end-to-end.
reason: The claim describes SciCode, a specific benchmark for research-level coding with hierarchical sub-problems, where frontier models solved <5% end-to-end. None of the candidates match this — they describe different benchmarks (SDE, ResearcherBench, BioMARS, FRI corpus) or evaluation studies of LLMs on biology/science tasks, none of which is SciCode.

claim: PaperBench (OpenAI, 2025) evaluated AI agents' ability to replicate AI research papers from scratch, finding that the best agents could successfully reproduce only a minority of paper results, establishing a concrete ceiling on current autonomous research capability.
reason: The claim describes PaperBench finding that the best agents could reproduce only a minority of paper results. Candidate k03 states frontier agents score at most 21.0% average replication on PaperBench, which is the same specific empirical finding about a concrete ceiling on autonomous research capability.

claim: ScienceAgentBench provided a benchmark of 102 tasks drawn from published scientific papers, requiring agents to write executable data-analysis code, and showed that even GPT-4o succeeded on fewer than 17% of tasks without additional scaffolding.
reason: The claim describes ScienceAgentBench with 102 tasks from peer-reviewed publications requiring executable data-analysis code — this matches the candidate which is ScienceAgentBench with 102 code-generation tasks from 44 peer-reviewed publications. The GPT-4o performance detail is an empirical finding from the same paper.

claim: CORE-Bench (2024) introduced a tiered benchmark for computational reproducibility across 270 papers in three disciplines, finding that top agents achieved ~21% success on the hardest tier that required full environment setup and re-running experiments.
reason: The claim describes CORE-Bench (2024), a benchmark across 270 papers in three disciplines with tiered difficulty. Candidate k00 matches exactly: 270 tasks from 90 papers across computer science, social science, and medicine at three difficulty levels. The 21% hardest-tier result is in k02, but the primary benchmark contribution described in the claim matches k00. However, the claim also specifically mentions the 21% finding — both k00 and k02 together describe CORE-Bench; k00 is the benchmark itself which is the primary referent of the claim.

claim: AAAR-1.0 introduced a four-task benchmark covering experiment design, review generation, and idea novelty assessment to systematically probe research workflow stages beyond code generation, revealing that LLMs struggle most with rigorous experimental design.
reason: The claim describes AAAR-1.0, a four-task benchmark covering experiment design, review generation, and idea novelty assessment. None of the candidates match this specific benchmark — the candidates describe different systems (VIRSCI, NovBench, MLR-Copilot, AFMBench, a six-agent pipeline, and an AI research agent success-rate finding), none of which is AAAR-1.0.

claim: ResearchClawBench (2026) proposed an end-to-end autonomous scientific research benchmark spanning 40 tasks from 10 scientific domains, each grounded in a real published paper with associated literature and raw data, finding no current system completes the full cycle reliably.
reason: The claim describes ResearchClawBench (2026), a benchmark spanning 40 tasks from 10 scientific domains grounded in real published papers with raw data. The closest candidate is AstaBench (arxiv:2510.21652v2::k00), a multi-domain benchmark for the full scientific discovery process, but it has 2400+ problems and different scope/design. No candidate matches the specific system named with its specific characteristics (40 tasks, 10 domains, each grounded in a paper with raw data).

claim: MLReplicate benchmarked autonomous research systems specifically on machine learning reproducibility, quantifying the gap between agent-generated experimental results and original reported numbers across a curated set of ML papers.
reason: The claim describes a benchmark called 'MLReplicate' focused on ML paper reproducibility. The closest candidate is arxiv:2504.01848v3::k03 (PaperBench), which also quantifies an agent-vs-human gap in ML replication, but PaperBench and MLReplicate are distinct benchmarks. No candidate matches the specific 'MLReplicate' system described in the claim.

claim: ScienceBoard (2025) evaluated multimodal autonomous agents in realistic scientific workflows covering bioinformatics, cheminformatics, and neuroscience tasks requiring tool use, file manipulation, and domain reasoning within authentic computational lab environments.
reason: The claim refers to 'ScienceBoard (2025)', a benchmark for multimodal autonomous agents in realistic scientific workflows. None of the candidates is ScienceBoard. The closest candidates are ScienceAgentBench (code-generation tasks, not multimodal agent evaluation in interactive lab environments) and SciAgentGym (interactive environment but different scope and name). No candidate describes the same specific benchmark system.

claim: PaperQA2 (Lala et al., 2024) showed that a retrieval-augmented LLM agent performing iterative literature search and synthesis could answer research questions at or above the accuracy of PhD-level human experts on a curated set of scientific questions, introducing the LitQA2 benchmark.
reason: The claim describes PaperQA2 matching or exceeding PhD-level expert performance on scientific QA tasks, which is exactly what k00 states. The LitQA2 benchmark mention is covered by k01 from the same paper, but k00 is the primary contribution the claim centers on (the expert-level performance finding). k00 from W4403795293 is the PaperQA2 paper, not the earlier PaperQA (W4389761608).

claim: The Denario project (2025) introduced deep knowledge AI agents grounded in structured domain ontologies for scientific discovery, demonstrating how graph-structured knowledge bases can constrain and guide hypothesis generation beyond what flat retrieval provides.
reason: This candidate describes a sub-graph sampling strategy that extracts subgraphs from large ontological knowledge graphs to guide multi-agent hypothesis generation — directly matching the claim's description of graph-structured knowledge bases constraining and guiding hypothesis generation via structured domain ontologies in a multi-agent scientific discovery setting.

claim: InternAgent (2025) implemented a closed-loop multi-agent system with specialized Survey Agents, Idea Innovation Agents, and Orchestration Agents that coordinate the full research loop from literature review through hypothesis formation to experimental verification, reporting significantly compressed timelines for biology experiments.
reason: The claim describes a specific system called 'InternAgent' with named agents (Survey Agents, Idea Innovation Agents, Orchestration Agents) focused on biology experiments. None of the candidates match this specific system — the closest are AI-Researcher (arxiv:2505.18705v1) and Denario (arxiv:2510.26887v1), but those are different named systems with different architectures. No candidate matches InternAgent specifically.

claim: ForeSci (2026) introduced a benchmark evaluating LLM agents on forward-looking AI research judgment—predicting which emerging research directions will prove impactful—finding that the best models correlate moderately with expert panels but systematically overestimate incremental over paradigm-shifting work.
reason: The claim describes ForeSci as a benchmark evaluating LLM agents on forward-looking AI research judgment, which matches candidate k00's description of ForeSci as a temporally controlled benchmark of 500 forward-looking AI research decision tasks spanning four AI domains and four decision families, with the same construction pipeline and forward-looking evaluation focus.

claim: AstaBench (2025) provided rigorous benchmarking of AI agents across a scientific research suite including literature comprehension, mathematical reasoning, and experimental planning, establishing standardized difficulty tiers and reporting that leading models saturate easy tiers but plateau below 30% on expert-level tasks.
reason: The claim describes AstaBench, a benchmark suite for scientific research tasks — matching the candidate which explicitly names AstaBench as a 2400+ problem suite spanning the full scientific discovery process. The claim's details about literature comprehension, mathematical reasoning, experimental planning, and difficulty tiers are consistent with this contribution's scope, though the specific 30% plateau figure is not in the candidate statement, the identity of the benchmark (name, scope, purpose) is clearly the same.

claim: The AI Scientist (Sakana AI, 2024) demonstrated fully automated open-ended scientific discovery: given a research area, the system generates ideas, runs experiments, and writes a manuscript with minimal human input, producing papers comparable to workshop-level submissions.
reason: This candidate describes the same AI Scientist system (Sakana AI) with the same key features: fully automated end-to-end pipeline, LLM generates ideas, runs experiments, writes manuscripts, performs self-review, at low cost per paper — matching the claim's description of automated open-ended scientific discovery producing workshop-level papers.

claim: The AI Scientist-v2 (2025) extended the original system with agentic tree search, enabling more systematic exploration of hypothesis spaces and achieving workshop-level automated scientific discovery across multiple ML subfields.
reason: The claim describes AI Scientist-v2 with agentic tree search for automated scientific discovery across ML subfields — this matches k00 exactly, which describes the same system with progressive agentic tree-search methodology and autonomous discovery across diverse ML domains.

claim: Agent Laboratory (2025, arXiv:2501.04227) introduced a framework using LLM agents as full research assistants, orchestrating ideation, coding, experimentation, and manuscript generation within a single pipeline.
reason: The claim describes Agent Laboratory (arXiv:2501.04227), a framework where LLM agents orchestrate the full research cycle including ideation, coding, experimentation, and manuscript generation. This matches candidate k00 exactly: Agent Laboratory is an autonomous LLM-based pipeline that accepts a research idea and completes literature review, experimentation, and report writing to produce a code repository and research report.

claim: PaperBench (Anthropic/OpenAI, 2025, arXiv:2504.01848) is a benchmark evaluating whether AI agents can replicate state-of-the-art AI research papers end-to-end, including re-implementing and re-running reported experiments.
reason: The claim describes PaperBench, the same benchmark from arXiv:2504.01848, which evaluates AI agents' ability to replicate AI research papers end-to-end. The candidate k00 from that same paper describes PaperBench with its ICML 2024 papers, rubrics, and grading workflow — a direct match.

claim: MLReplicate (2025, arXiv:2605.16616) is a benchmark specifically focused on machine learning reproducibility, testing autonomous research systems on their ability to faithfully reproduce published ML results.
reason: The claim describes MLReplicate, a benchmark for ML reproducibility testing autonomous systems on reproducing published ML results. None of the candidates match this specific system. The closest is arxiv:2606.13670::k00 (an agentic pipeline for reproducibility assessment), but that is a pipeline for reproducing statistical claims from articles, not a benchmark called MLReplicate focused on ML result reproduction. The Weco Kaggle Benchmark is about ML engineering, not reproducibility. No candidate describes the same specific benchmark.

claim: ResearchGym (2025, arXiv:2602.15112) is a benchmark suite that evaluates LLM agents on real-world AI research tasks across the full research lifecycle, including idea generation, implementation, and evaluation.
reason: The claim describes ResearchGym from arXiv:2602.15112, and candidate k00 from that same paper describes ResearchGym as an execution environment and benchmark for AI agents on closed-loop research tasks — same system, same paper.

claim: BioDisco (2025, arXiv:2508.01285) is a multi-agent system for biomedical hypothesis generation that uses dual-mode evidence retrieval, iterative feedback loops, and temporal holdout evaluation to assess discovery novelty.
reason: BioDisco is a specific biomedical hypothesis generation system with dual-mode evidence retrieval and temporal holdout evaluation. None of the candidates match: they describe different systems (ACCELMAT for materials, Robin for lab-in-the-loop, PaperQA for RAG QA, KGCheck for KG error detection, AI Scientist-v2 for ML research automation, AI co-scientist on Gemini 2.0). No candidate corresponds to BioDisco specifically.

claim: HypoBench (2025) is a systematic benchmark for hypothesis generation that tests LLMs on generating and ranking scientific hypotheses across disciplines, providing principled evaluation metrics beyond novelty.
reason: The claim describes HypoBench as a systematic benchmark for hypothesis generation with evaluation metrics beyond novelty. The candidate openalex:W4416540539::k00 is exactly HypoBench, a benchmark spanning 194 datasets across 7 real-world and 5 synthetic tasks that evaluates hypothesis generation along multiple dimensions including practical utility, generalizability, and ground-truth discovery rate — matching the claim's description of principled evaluation metrics beyond novelty.

claim: InternAgent (2025) is a closed-loop multi-agent system that coordinates Survey Agents (literature review), Idea Innovation Agents (hypothesis generation), and Orchestration Agents (workflow management) to automate the full research pipeline.
reason: The claim describes a specific system called 'InternAgent' with named agent types (Survey Agents, Idea Innovation Agents, Orchestration Agents). None of the candidates describe InternAgent — they describe Robin, AI-Researcher, a review pipeline, Agent Laboratory, KBase Research Agent, and Denario. While several are similar multi-agent research automation systems, none is the same specific system described in the claim.

claim: AI-Researcher (2025, arXiv:2505.18705) demonstrated autonomous orchestration of the entire research pipeline—from literature survey to manuscript preparation—achieving near-human-quality paper output with high implementation success rates.
reason: The claim explicitly references arXiv:2505.18705 (AI-Researcher) and describes a fully autonomous multi-agent system orchestrating the complete research pipeline from literature review to manuscript preparation, which is exactly what candidate arxiv:2505.18705v1::k00 describes.

claim: SciAtlas (2025, arXiv:2605.22878) is a large-scale scientific knowledge graph designed to support automated research by mapping entities and relations across millions of papers, enabling structured retrieval for literature agents.
reason: The claim describes SciAtlas, a large-scale scientific knowledge graph for automated research with structured retrieval for literature agents. None of the candidates describe this system — they cover a hypothesis generation benchmark, BioKGBench/KGCheck for biomedical agents, BKGAgent, an LLM review corpus, and object-graph mapping for workflows.

claim: A 2025 analysis of ICLR 2025 submissions found that approximately 21% of the roughly 70,000 peer reviews were fully AI-generated, establishing an empirical baseline for LLM penetration into academic peer review.
reason: The claim describes an empirical study finding ~21% of ~70,000 ICLR 2025 peer reviews were fully AI-generated. None of the candidates match this specific finding — the candidates cover a synthetic review corpus, benchmarks, fine-tuned models, and an AI-generated manuscript experiment, but none report an analysis quantifying AI-generated review penetration at ICLR 2025.

claim: NeurIPS 2026 launched a voluntary AI-assisted reviewing experiment to study how different forms of LLM assistance affect review quality and reviewer behavior, marking the first large-scale controlled trial of AI in a top-tier venue's review process.
reason: The claim describes a specific NeurIPS 2026 controlled experiment on AI-assisted reviewing. None of the candidates describe this specific initiative — they cover related but different contributions: AI-generated manuscripts passing review, review critique datasets, multi-agent review frameworks, and research benchmarks.

claim: ForeSci (2025, arXiv:2606.00644) is a benchmark that evaluates LLM agents on forward-looking AI research judgment—predicting which ideas will prove impactful—testing prospective reasoning rather than retrospective knowledge.
reason: The claim describes ForeSci as a benchmark evaluating LLM agents on forward-looking AI research judgment with prospective reasoning. Candidate k00 is exactly that: a temporally controlled benchmark of 500 forward-looking AI research decision tasks from arXiv:2606.00644, matching the same system and paper.

claim: Act As a Real Researcher benchmark suite (2025, arXiv:2606.07462) evaluates frontier LLMs and agentic harnesses across the full research lifecycle (literature search, hypothesis formation, experiment design, writing), finding that current agents still fall short of expert human researchers on open-ended tasks.
reason: The claim describes a benchmark evaluating LLMs across the full research lifecycle (literature search, hypothesis formation, experiment design, writing). Candidate W7118690722::k02 defines exactly this full research cycle spanning literature understanding, hypothesis formulation, implementation, experiment execution, validation, interpretation, and writing as a holistic evaluation framework — a strong identity match on the same specific contribution.

claim: FutureHouse's Robin (2025) is a multi-agent drug discovery system that orchestrates specialized sub-agents across literature review, hypothesis generation, and experiment design, and also interfaces with real-world laboratory hardware to analyze experimental data.
reason: The claim describes Robin, a multi-agent drug discovery system from FutureHouse with specialized sub-agents for literature review, hypothesis generation, experiment design, and interfacing with laboratory hardware. This matches candidate W4417287919::k00, which describes Robin as a multi-agent system with literature search agents (Crow, Falcon) and an experimental data analysis agent (Finch) for autonomous scientific discovery in a lab-in-the-loop framework.

claim: ChemCrow (Bran et al., Nature Machine Intelligence 2024) is an LLM augmented with 18 expert-designed chemistry tools—including RDKit, PubChem, and retrosynthesis engines—that autonomously plans and executes chemical synthesis tasks bridging drug discovery and materials science.
reason: ChemCrow is a specific LLM system augmented with 18 chemistry tools for synthesis planning. None of the candidates describe ChemCrow or the same system — the closest candidates describe other multi-agent chemistry systems (El Agente Q, Intelligent Agent) or benchmarks/frameworks that are clearly distinct contributions.

claim: Sakana AI's 'The AI Scientist' (Lu et al., 2024) demonstrated the first end-to-end autonomous research system capable of generating ideas, writing and executing code, analyzing results, and producing full research papers with minimal human involvement, and its v2 (2025) produced the first fully AI-generated paper accepted at an ICLR 2025 workshop.
reason: This candidate directly describes The AI Scientist (Lu et al., 2024) as a fully automated end-to-end pipeline that generates ideas, writes and executes code, runs experiments, writes manuscripts, and performs self-review — matching the claim's description of the first end-to-end autonomous research system. The ICLR 2025 workshop acceptance aspect of v2 is covered by a separate candidate (k01), but the primary thrust of the claim is the v1 system's capabilities, which this candidate matches most closely.

claim: ScienceAgentBench (OSU NLP Group, ICLR 2025) provides 102 data-driven scientific discovery tasks sourced from 44 peer-reviewed papers and validated by expert PhD students; OpenAI o3 Medium achieved only 33.3% accuracy as of April 2025, highlighting large remaining gaps.
reason: The claim describes ScienceAgentBench as a benchmark of 102 data-driven scientific discovery tasks sourced from 44 peer-reviewed papers and validated by expert PhD students, which directly matches candidate k00's description of ScienceAgentBench with 102 code-generation tasks from 44 peer-reviewed publications across four scientific disciplines, each validated by subject matter experts.

claim: PaperBench (OpenAI, 2025) evaluates AI agents on their ability to replicate AI research papers end-to-end—implementing methods, running experiments, and producing aligned artifacts—with the best agentic scaffold achieving only 21.0%, establishing a difficult reproducibility benchmark.
reason: The claim specifically describes PaperBench with AI agents achieving 21.0% replication, which matches k03 exactly — same benchmark, same score, same finding about the gap between AI and human capability.

claim: BioPlanner (2023–2024) introduced automatic evaluation of LLMs on biology protocol planning tasks, formalizing assessment of step-by-step experimental procedure generation in a domain requiring precise procedural knowledge.
reason: BioPlanner is about automatic evaluation of LLMs on biology protocol planning (step-by-step experimental procedure generation). None of the candidates match this specific system. LAB-Bench (W4402345509) is the closest topic-wise (biology AI benchmark) but covers multiple-choice questions across broad biology research tasks, not protocol planning specifically. The others are about scientific code generation, peer review, memory retrieval, research-idea generation, or computational biology — all different systems.

claim: Google's AI co-scientist system (2025) applies a multi-agent architecture with a supervisor agent coordinating specialized sub-agents for literature review, hypothesis generation, and experimental critique, and was demonstrated on biomedical research contexts.
reason: The claim describes Google's AI co-scientist (2025), a multi-agent system built on Gemini 2.0 with specialized agents for literature review, hypothesis generation, and experimental critique in biomedical contexts. This matches the candidate describing an AI co-scientist built on Gemini 2.0 with specialized agents (Generation, Reflection, Ranking, Evolution, etc.) for formulating novel research hypotheses.

claim: FIRE-Bench (2025, arXiv:2602.02905) evaluates AI agents on the rediscovery of documented scientific insights, measuring whether agents can autonomously recover known findings from data—providing a ground-truth-verifiable scientific reasoning benchmark.
reason: FIRE-Bench evaluates AI agents on rediscovery of documented scientific insights from data — a ground-truth-verifiable benchmark focused on recovering known findings. None of the candidates describe this specific system or approach. The candidates cover genomics workflows (GeneBench), AI research reproduction (Scientist-Bench, EXP-Bench), bioinformatics planning (KBase), combustion LLM agents (FlamePilot), and clinical hypothesis grounding — none match FIRE-Bench's specific rediscovery-of-findings framing.

claim: ReplicationBench (2025, arXiv:2510.24591) specifically tests whether AI agents can replicate astrophysics research papers, combining code generation, data retrieval, and numerical analysis in a domain-specific end-to-end reproducibility challenge.
reason: The claim describes ReplicationBench, a benchmark specifically for astrophysics paper replication by AI agents. The closest candidate is arxiv:2507.18901v1::k00 (REPRO-Bench), which is also an AI agent reproducibility benchmark, but it targets social science papers — a different domain, different paper set, and different task design. claim: ResearchClawBench (2025, arXiv:2606.07591) introduces an end-to-end autonomous scientific research benchmark assessing the full pipeline from literature retrieval through hypothesis formulation to result generation.

The drug-repurposing aspect is also validated by k03 from the same paper, but k00 is the primary system contribution matching the claim's focus on the multi-agent architecture with iterative self-critique.

The Popperian framing aligns with this candidate's emphasis on falsification as the core contribution.

claim: Chan et al. (2024) developed ScienceAgentBench, a benchmark of 102 real scientific data-analysis programming tasks drawn from peer-reviewed papers, finding that the best agents succeed on fewer than 34% of tasks, highlighting the difficulty of authentic scientific coding.
reason: The claim specifically highlights that the best agents succeed on fewer than 34% of ScienceAgentBench tasks. Candidate k03 from the same paper reports exactly this: 'at most 32.4% unaided and 34.3% with expert-provided knowledge', which directly corresponds to the <34% figure cited in the claim. The benchmark description (102 tasks, peer-reviewed papers) is covered by k00, but the distinctive empirical finding about agent success rate matches k03.

claim: FutureHouse (2025) released Robin, a multi-agent system integrating literature search, hypothesis generation, experimental planning, and result interpretation that autonomously identified and validated a novel mechanism for antibiotic resistance, reported as the first end-to-end AI-driven biological discovery.
reason: The claim describes Robin, a multi-agent system from FutureHouse integrating literature search, hypothesis generation, experimental planning, and result interpretation for autonomous biological discovery. This matches the candidate exactly: Robin with its Crow/Falcon/Finch agents for the same workflow, described as the first end-to-end AI system for autonomous scientific discovery in a lab-in-the-loop framework.

claim: Starace et al. (OpenAI, 2025) introduced PaperBench, a benchmark that tests AI agents on replicating 20 ICML 2024 papers from scratch, with the strongest agent achieving only 26% replication fidelity and revealing systematic failures in experiment design and debugging.
reason: The claim describes PaperBench, a benchmark of 20 ICML 2024 papers for evaluating AI agents on replicating AI research from scratch. This matches k00 exactly — same benchmark name, same paper set, same core purpose. The cited replication figure (26%) is slightly off from k03's 21.0% max, but the primary contribution being introduced is the benchmark itself, which is k00.

claim: Guo et al. (2024) presented a comprehensive survey of agentic AI for scientific discovery, taxonomising autonomous research systems across hypothesis generation, experiment execution, literature synthesis, and evaluation, and cataloguing open challenges in reliability, hallucination, and reproducibility.
reason: The claim describes a survey paper by Guo et al. (2024) that taxonomises autonomous research systems across multiple phases and catalogs open challenges. None of the candidates match this — they describe specific systems (Denario, MLR-Copilot, ACCELMAT), benchmarks, or risk taxonomies derived from system deployment, not a broad survey taxonomising the field.

claim: Jansen et al. (AAAR-1.0, 2024) proposed a benchmark that assesses LLM agents on experiment design, equipment planning, and result review in an authentic chemistry-lab setting, demonstrating that current models lack the structured procedural reasoning required for rigorous experimental science.
reason: The claim describes AAAR-1.0, a benchmark specifically for chemistry-lab tasks (experiment design, equipment planning, result review). None of the candidates match this specific system. The closest candidates are general multi-domain benchmarks (SDE, EXP-Bench) or agent systems, but none is AAAR-1.0 focused on authentic chemistry-lab procedural reasoning.

claim: Baek et al. (2024) presented ResearchAgent, a system that mines an academic knowledge graph to generate, iteratively refine, and evaluate research ideas, showing that entity-grounded retrieval substantially improves novelty and feasibility scores.
reason: The claim describes ResearchAgent by Baek et al. (2024), which mines an academic knowledge graph to generate and iteratively refine research ideas evaluated for novelty and feasibility. This matches candidate k00 exactly: an LLM system augmenting papers with an academic-graph neighbourhood and entity-centric knowledge store, refined via ReviewingAgents.

claim: Jin et al. (2024) released AgentReview, an LLM-based simulation of the full NeurIPS peer-review process (authors, reviewers, area chairs) that reproduces known social biases and enables controlled study of review dynamics at scale.
reason: The claim describes AgentReview, an LLM-based simulation of the full peer-review process with authors, reviewers, and area chairs, which exactly matches the first candidate's description of AgentReview as an LLM agent-based simulation framework modeling the full peer review pipeline with customizable reviewer, author, and area-chair characteristics.
